forecasts.reasoning: The threshold is extremely high. Using the provided reference value of 9.833, Honduras would need roughly 99+ ACLED 'Strategic developments' in a 30-day window to resolve YES. My outside view is that this is a very tail outcome for a single country-month, especially because ACLED codes much election turmoil under other event types (violence against civilians, protests, riots) rather than 'Strategic developments.' Searches do show a tense 2025 election environment—candidate killings, gridlock, judicial concerns, logistical failures, and warnings of possible post-election conflict—but not clear evidence of the kind of massive non-violent strategic-event surge needed to get near 100. The 2025-11-02 window is lowest because most of that window had already elapsed by the cutoff with no sign of an extraordinary spike. The 2025-11-25 window is still pre-election, so somewhat higher but still low. The 2026-01-24 window is highest because a disputed result, certification fight, or pre-inauguration institutional crisis could prolong strategic developments into late Dec-Jan, though the bar remains very high.

Monaco is a very low-incidence case for ACLED riots, and the stated recent reference average was 0.000 as of 2025-10-16. That makes this question close to asking whether Monaco will record at least one riot in each target 30-day window. The evidence I found points to occasional demonstrations and strikes in Monaco, but they were small and peaceful; recent labor unrest also often played out in nearby French cities rather than Monaco itself. October 2025 coverage showed political disagreement and active policing, but no sign of violent unrest. So the near-term window is at the floor, with only a modest increase for later dates due to more time for unforeseen disturbances or backfilled events.

The hurdle is likely about 11+ ACLED 'Strategic developments' in a 30-day window, because the latest known reference value remained 1.000 in mid-October 2025. Lesotho does have chronic elite political instability and an active 2025 dispute over the Tenth Amendment, including court challenges and criticism of the government's presentation to SADC. But the direct late-2025 evidence I found looks like a small number of legal and political disputes, not a dense, cascading crisis. That makes a double-digit monthly spike possible but unlikely. The Nov 2 date is especially unlikely because most of its 30-day window had already effectively shown zero strategic developments by mid-October; Nov 25 and Jan 24 are a bit higher only because more unobserved time allows for a sudden crisis, and uncertainty rises with horizon.

The fixed comparison bar is very high: about 128.25 ACLED protest events per 30 days (~4.3/day). Israel clearly still had a dense protest environment through Aug-Oct 2025, with nationwide hostage/end-war rallies, recurring Tel Aviv actions, anti-government mobilization, and additional ultra-Orthodox draft protests. That keeps all dates live. But the most important late-breaking evidence is that a Gaza ceasefire/hostage process was ratified around Oct. 10 and was still being actively sustained in late October. Since hostage/end-war protests were one of the biggest drivers of Israel’s protest count, that likely pulls the post-cutoff November windows somewhat below the already-elevated trailing-year average. For the farthest date, uncertainty rises and I pull back toward 0.5 because ceasefire durability is unclear and domestic accountability / draft fights could rebuild protest volume.

The effective threshold is about 12 ACLED-coded explosions/remote-violence events in a 30-day window, based on the current 11.25 trailing-year monthly average. Recent evidence is directly supportive: multiple FARDC drone strikes/airstrikes and other remote-violence incidents were documented across late September and October 2025, including repeated strikes on M23 positions in North and South Kivu, showing this event type was active right up to the forecast date. That makes the nearest date, 2025-11-02, more likely than not to resolve YES because its 30-day window overlaps almost entirely with this already-hot period. For 2025-11-25, I still lean YES, but less strongly, because the late-October Doha ceasefire and monitoring mechanisms could begin to suppress air/drone strikes and shelling during November even if frontlines remained volatile. For 2026-01-24, I move closer to 0.5: eastern DRC conflict remains structurally prone to remote violence, but over a three-month horizon there is much more room for mean reversion, episodic lulls, or partial diplomatic de-escalation, so uncertainty is higher and the estimate should be closer to even.

Baseline is about 3.167 protests per 30 days, so the operative hurdle is likely 4+ ACLED-coded protest events in the target 30-day window. My outside-view starting point was therefore just under 40%. I adjust a bit downward for the November dates because the 360-day baseline was probably inflated by the exceptional Sep-Oct 2024 cost-of-living protest wave, and by late Sep/Oct 2025 RPPRAC appeared to be shifting some energy from street protest into party-building and electoral politics. That said, I do not go very low: local reporting shows RPPRAC still had mobilization capacity in 2025, grievances remained unresolved, and there were multiple labor disputes and strike/mobilization calls in Sep-Oct 2025. Net: somewhat below the raw base rate for the nearest window, slightly higher for late November because it is a fresh unknown month with ongoing unrest risk, and closer to 0.5 by late January due greater uncertainty.

The bar is extremely high. Using the prompt’s latest known reference value (~3.167), Hungary would need roughly 32+ ACLED 'Strategic developments' in a 30-day window, versus a recent implied pace of only about 2.2 per 30 days. Searches suggest ACLED can code nonviolent political/legal acts, and Hungary was in a tense pre-election environment with opposition momentum, rival rallies, and sovereignty/NGO legislation. But as of 2025-10-26, the available evidence looks more like polarized campaigning and some legal escalation than the kind of acute crackdown, border emergency, or security shock usually needed for a >10x surge. So I assign a floor-level chance for 2025-11-02, a slightly higher chance for 2025-11-25, and a somewhat higher—but still low—chance for 2026-01-24 because it is farther out and closer to the 2026 election.

This question is effectively asking whether there will be at least one ACLED-coded riot in Akrotiri and Dhekelia in each target 30-day window. The reported reference value was 0.000 as of 2025-10-16, and even modest backfill would still usually leave the practical threshold at 1 riot. The territory is tiny and usually quiet, which is a strong outside-view negative. The main plausible mechanism is anti-base / pro-Palestinian mobilization around RAF Akrotiri: there were repeated demonstrations in 2024-2025, including several-hundred-person protests, and activist scrutiny continued into October 2025. But the evidence found consistently describes these as protests and demonstrations, not riots, and I found no explicit riot in the late-September to October 2025 period. That especially suppresses the Nov. 2 forecast, since most of that window had already elapsed by the cutoff without evidence of disorder. I raise the later-date probabilities somewhat because uncertainty is higher and activist networks remain active, but they still stay low given the absence of a recent riot pattern.

The threshold is extremely hard to clear: with the reported reference value at 1.000 as of 2025-10-16, YES effectively requires 11 or more ACLED battle events in a 30-day window in a country that appears to have had essentially no recent battle activity. Recent reporting around Solomon Islands focuses on unrest, riots, and policing rather than active organized armed-group combat. Reuters described the April 2024 election period as mostly calm despite tensions, and September 2025 coverage centered on surveillance and policing to curb social unrest, not an ongoing militia conflict. That keeps the first two dates near the floor. I raise the January 2026 probability somewhat because longer horizons allow more chance of an unexpected political or ethnic flare-up, but it still remains low given the very high bar of 11+ battles in a month.

The reference average was recently 0.500 fatalities per 30 days, so the practical threshold is very likely 1 fatality in the target window unless ACLED backfill is unexpectedly large. Reuters reported no border fatalities since Feb. 2024 despite recurring ceasefire accusations, and 2025 also saw peace-process progress, which keeps the main interstate pathway to a YES relatively subdued. Still, Azerbaijan retains a persistent background risk from mines/explosive incidents in retaken territories, including a fatal April 2025 mine/ammunition incident. For 2025-11-02, most of the 30-day window had already elapsed by the cutoff and I found no clear fatal incident in that partially observed period, so the near-term probability is notably lower. For later dates, I revert toward the roughly one-fatality-per-few-months base rate, with farther horizons closer to 0.5 as uncertainty rises.

The threshold is very high relative to Canada’s recent baseline. The prompt’s own reference value of 1.083 implies only about one qualifying ACLED 'Explosions/Remote violence' event in the prior 360 days, so a YES likely requires roughly 11 events in a 30-day window (possibly 12 if the reference value drifted up slightly). Searches up to 2025-10-26 found an elevated terrorism threat environment and some isolated firebombing/incendiary incidents, but mostly foiled plots, arrests, and isolated cases rather than any realized nationwide campaign of bombings or remote attacks. That makes an 11+ event monthly spike unlikely. The 2025-11-02 date is especially unlikely because most of that 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, leaving very little time for a huge surge. I raise the later-date probabilities modestly because there is more time for conditions to change or for a trigger event to produce clustering, but they remain low because the required jump from baseline is extreme.

The key tradeoff is that Mozambique was still experiencing two active generators of ACLED 'Strategic developments' as of late Oct. 2025: persistent post-election political unrest and an acute Cabo Delgado insurgency. That pushes the odds above 50% that a 30-day window will clear the threshold. But the threshold itself is already high — the trailing 360-day average was about 20.2 events per 30 days, so resolution effectively needs roughly 21+ events — meaning current turmoil may only match, not necessarily exceed, the recent average. I therefore put the near-term window modestly above even, then pull later dates back toward 0.5 because uncertainty rises and mean reversion/stabilization becomes more plausible.

The implied threshold is extremely high: with the reference value near 10.5, resolution requires roughly 106+ ACLED 'Violence against civilians' events in a 30-day window. Venezuela clearly remains repressive, and authoritative reporting shows a brutal post-2024-election crackdown that continued into 2025, but the public quantitative summaries I found emphasize dozens of killings and mass detentions rather than something that clearly maps to 100+ discrete VAC events in a month. For 2025-11-02, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the forecast date, and there was no strong evidence of an active nationwide surge, so I put this near the floor. For 2025-11-25, the probability is only slightly higher because major 2025 elections had already passed. For 2026-01-24, risk rises modestly due to the January 2026 National Assembly transition and talk of constitutional reform, which could provoke protests and repression, but the threshold still looks hard to clear.

I end slightly below even odds. The best concrete fatality-rate evidence I found is HRSS’s 160 political-violence deaths from Sep 2024-Sep 2025, which annualizes well below the ~31-fatality 30-day ACLED benchmark, suggesting the reference level may be elevated by earlier spikes. Bangladesh was still clearly unstable in October 2025, with party exclusion, protests, and Hill Tracts violence, but I found little evidence that the most salient October clashes inside the first forecast window were highly lethal. So the near-term window looks a bit more likely than not to stay below the benchmark; later dates move back toward 0.5 because new triggers could emerge and uncertainty is higher.

Mongolia’s baseline ACLED fatality level is essentially zero, so this question needs an unusually sharp break from normal patterns: roughly >10 fatalities in a 30-day window. Recent evidence shows genuine political instability in 2025 — corruption protests, a prime ministerial resignation, and an October constitutional crisis — but the best contemporary reporting points to protests being peaceful or nonviolent, with no clear sign of deaths. Even Mongolia’s notable historical unrest benchmark in 2008 reportedly caused five deaths, still below this threshold. For 2025-11-02, most of the relevant 30-day window had already elapsed by the knowledge cutoff, and there was still no visible evidence of lethal escalation, so I put it at the floor. I raise probabilities modestly for the later dates because there is more time for elite instability to spill into the streets, but Mongolia remains a low-conflict country and a >10-fatality month would still require a substantial, unusual escalation.

The fixed benchmark is very high at roughly 435 protest events in 30 days, and Reuters reported in July that the huge post-Imamoglu spring protest wave had already abated. That makes beating the annual average somewhat less likely than not, especially because ACLED counts distinct protest events rather than crowd size, so a few very large rallies do not guarantee a high monthly total. Still, Reuters also showed sustained opposition mobilization into September and continuing crackdowns that could generate renewed protest waves, so I stay only modestly below 50%; for the more distant dates I move closer to 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty and the possibility of new triggers.

The effective threshold is very likely at least one ACLED-coded Explosions/Remote violence event in the 30-day window, because the latest known rolling baseline was only 0.083 per 30 days. Chile appears to be a low-frequency setting for this event type: I found one confirmed 2025 explosion in Providencia in May, but targeted searches did not uncover comparable confirmed events in late October 2025; an October 20 airport bomb alert was a false alarm, and much recent southern violence appears to be incendiary/arson rather than clearly ACLED-coded remote violence. So the first date is quite unlikely, while later dates are slightly higher mainly because of greater uncertainty about new incidents emerging over longer horizons.

The dominant fact is the baseline itself: as of 2025-10-16, the reference value was 1.083, which implies only about one ACLED 'Strategic developments' event in New Caledonia over the prior 360 days. So a YES likely requires roughly 10-11+ strategic developments in a single 30-day window, an enormous jump from recent coding frequency. Searches do show real catalysts: the Bougival Accord is contested, the electoral roll dispute continues, and late-October 2025 brought several institutional moves around postponing provincial elections, with FLNKS warnings about renewed unrest. But that still looks more like a small number of high-level political steps than the kind of acute, many-step security/political crisis needed to generate 11 separately coded strategic developments in one month. Hence I keep all dates below 20%, with slightly higher probabilities at longer horizons because there is more time for the election-delay dispute or accord-implementation fight to escalate.

The key threshold is roughly 4.75 strategic developments per 30 days, so each window likely needs about 5+ ACLED-coded strategic developments. For 2025-11-02, much of the relevant window is already visible by the forecast date and contains a dense cluster of election-related administrative and political steps: repeated rally bans, opposition protest calls/boycott activity, heavy security posture, and then provisional results shortly after the vote. That makes an above-baseline month more likely than not. For 2025-11-25, the pre-election cluster drops out and the window relies mainly on results, certification, and any opposition response; that still supports a modestly elevated probability, but calm election-day reporting and expectations of a comfortable first-round Ouattara win cap it. By 2026-01-24, most immediate election mechanics should be past, so I regress close to the base rate, with only a slight residual tilt upward from possible post-election/inauguration-related political developments.

This looks like an extreme-tail event. Historical/background sources say Antarctica is effectively the only continent with no war history, and multiple institutional sources state the Antarctic Treaty demilitarizes the area south of 60°S and reserves it for peaceful purposes. Recent 2025 analysis still describes those norms as durable rather than collapsing. The most recent known reference value was 1.000 as of 2025-10-16, so the question likely needs roughly 11+ ACLED-coded battle events in a 30-day window to resolve YES. That is a massive jump from an apparent recent baseline near zero. I leave a small nonzero tail for ACLED geography/coding quirks, data revisions, or an unexpected maritime/fisheries/sovereignty incident, and that tail grows modestly with time, hence slightly higher probabilities for later dates.

Reference class: short-horizon moves in a daily corporate-vs-Treasury spread series. The FRED time-series model is the main anchor and puts the chance of being above the latest observed threshold near 52% for the first date. I then make a small qualitative adjustment from contemporaneous market context: late-2025 investment-grade spreads were extremely tight by historical standards, which creates some asymmetry toward modest re-widening, but strong demand/flows were also still supporting tight spreads. Net: only a slight lean to YES near term, fading back toward 50/50 at longer horizons as uncertainty dominates.

Primary anchor is the FRED time-series estimate: with the latest pre-cutoff value at 4.38 on 2025-10-24, the tool’s combined statistical estimate gave a 0.62 chance that the 2025-11-02 value would be above that threshold. I keep the nearest-date forecast close to that anchor. I then shade slightly downward because late-October market tone included falling Treasury yields and very tight credit spreads, both of which work against a rise in AA corporate yields. Offsetting that, Reuters polling and credit commentary suggested long Treasury yields were expected to stay somewhat elevated over coming months and corporate spreads had some room to widen from very tight levels. Net: modestly more likely than not that the index yield is higher by early November, still somewhat more likely by late November, and only slightly more likely by late January. Per instruction, uncertainty increases with horizon, so the later probabilities move closer to 0.5.

Primary anchor is the FRED time-series estimate: using data through 2025-10-24, it gives about a 0.65 chance that the BBB effective yield will be above 4.88 by 2025-11-02. I trim that a bit because the true 2025-10-26 comparison value is not yet known and could be a couple of basis points higher than 4.88. The one web search adds a mild upward tilt: late-2025 market commentary pointed to sticky inflation / stronger growth than the market was pricing, Treasury yields staying firm, and BBB/IG spreads already very tight, limiting further spread compression. Offsetting that, the Fed was still expected to cut further and strong demand for IG credit could keep spreads tight. Net: slightly better-than-even odds of being higher in early November, then probabilities fade back toward 0.5 as horizon lengthens and uncertainty rises.

Primary anchor is the FRED CC4WSA trend model, which gave a near-coin-flip 0.47 chance for 2025-11-02 because the late-October comparison level is already near a recent high. I nudge a bit above that because the recent series trend is upward, recent momentum was positive, and late-September/October Reuters reporting described continuing claims as elevated and rising amid softer hiring. Still, this is only a 4-week moving average call relative to a high benchmark, so the edge is small. For the later dates I pull the forecast back toward 0.5, both because of the instruction to reflect greater horizon uncertainty and because seasonal-adjustment noise and broader macro uncertainty make direction versus the late-October level hard to call months out.

Primary anchor is the FRED tool’s statistical estimate for CCLACBW027SBOG, which put the 2025-11-02 probability of being above the current threshold at 0.55. I nudge that slightly up to 0.56 because contemporaneous macro evidence before the cutoff pointed to resilient consumers, rising card spend, rebounding bankcard originations, and continued balance growth. For 2025-11-25, I keep the probability only modestly above even odds at 0.55: late-November holiday borrowing is a tailwind, but the series’ recent momentum was flat and longer-horizon uncertainty should pull the estimate closer to 0.5. For 2026-01-24, I move slightly below even odds to 0.48 because the sampled FRED history shows meaningful post-holiday declines into January, even though broader 2025 credit conditions were stable. The biggest unresolved issue is the exact comparison value on 2025-10-26, which appears somewhat ambiguous in the prompt; that keeps all estimates fairly close to 0.5.

Primary anchor is the FRED time-series analysis, which directly estimated only a 0.36 chance of exceeding the latest known threshold by 2025-11-02. The series has been drifting upward over the past year, but very slowly, with meaningful week-to-week noise and a flat recent momentum signal. That makes the nearest date slightly unlikely to be above the 2025-10-26 comparison level, especially since the baseline appears to be near a local high. For later dates, I raise the probability modestly because the underlying trend is still positive and industry reporting suggested lending/origination activity had rebounded earlier in 2025, but I keep all horizons below 50% because growth had also slowed materially and there is some evidence of seasonal softness into late year/early year. Per instruction, the longer-horizon estimates move closer to 0.5 as uncertainty rises.

Primary anchor is the FRED trend analysis for D2WLTGAL as of the 2025-10-26 cutoff: with the latest known observation at 905,074 on 2025-10-22, the combined statistical estimate for being above that level by 2025-11-02 is about 0.33. I keep the nearest-date forecast very close to that anchor. Official Treasury guidance adds two offsetting considerations: on the bullish side, Treasury said it is still rebuilding the cash balance over time after the July debt-limit increase, and it can use bill/CMB issuance to manage quarter-to-quarter cash needs; on the bearish side, Treasury's published end-December 2025 cash-balance assumption is $850B, which is below the current ~905B comparison level. So I stay below 50% for all dates, but because forecast uncertainty should widen with horizon, I move the later probabilities gradually back toward 0.5.

Reference class: short-horizon directional moves in the 6-month Treasury yield, which are driven mostly by changes in expected Fed policy over the next 6 months. My primary anchor is the FRED time-series output: as of 2025-10-24 the yield was 3.76, and the recent-statistical model gave about a 0.68 chance of being above that level by 2025-11-02. But I adjusted that down using contemporaneous macro evidence. Reuters reported that 115 of 117 economists expected a 25 bp Fed cut on Oct. 29, and that short-dated Treasury yields were expected to edge lower on Fed-cut expectations. J.P. Morgan similarly said short rates should fall as the Fed cuts, and Morningstar described policy-rate expectations drifting lower into end-2025 and 2026. That makes an increase less likely than the raw time-series prior, especially by 2025-11-25 and 2026-01-24. I still keep the nearest date slightly above 50% because the yield had already fallen sharply into late October, some easing was likely already priced, the exact 2025-10-26 comparison value is uncertain, and Reuters also emphasized that the 2026 path was very unclear. So: modestly above even for 11/02, modestly below even for 11/25, and then slightly closer to 50% by 1/24 due to greater horizon uncertainty.

My anchor is the FRED DJIA trend analysis through 2025-10-24, which gives about a 0.31 chance of being above the effective threshold (the 2025-10-24 close of 47,207.12, since 2025-10-26 is a non-trading day) by 2025-11-02. I adjust that up slightly because contemporaneous Reuters reporting showed a supportive backdrop: cooler-than-expected inflation, strong earnings beats, and expectations for a Fed cut. But the threshold is a fresh all-time high after a sharp rally, and the following week had major Fed and earnings event risk, so I keep the near-term probability below 40%. For 2025-11-25 and 2026-01-24, I move the probability upward toward 0.5 because equities have positive drift over longer horizons, yet I still keep them below 0.5 because the comparison point is already a record high and there was meaningful macro/event uncertainty.

Primary anchor is the FRED history for DPSACBW027SBOG through 2025-10-22. That series shows a clear 6–12 month upward drift (from about 17,832 a year earlier to about 18,502 most recently), but short-run weekly moves are noisy and the last five observations are mixed. So I land only modestly above 50% for all dates. Per horizon uncertainty, I pull the farther dates closer to 0.5.

I anchored on the pre-cutoff DTB4WK time-series estimate, which slightly favored an increase by 2025-11-02 versus the apparent late-October level. But I adjusted down from that prior because, by 2025-10-26, Reuters polling showed an overwhelming expectation of a 25 bp Fed cut at the next meeting and another in December, and Reuters also reported expectations that short-dated Treasury yields would edge lower. The reason I do not go much below 50% for the nearest date is that the 4-week bill rate had already dropped sharply into the cutoff, so a lot of near-term easing likely was already priced. By 2025-11-25, one cut should be in place and the 4-week bill will also reflect the expected December cut, so an increase versus 2025-10-26 looks slightly less likely than not. By 2026-01-24, cumulative easing still argues against an increase, but the 2026 path was described as very unclear and sticky inflation/hawkish caution could keep front-end yields from falling much further, so I pull the far-horizon forecast back toward 0.5. A major uncertainty is the exact official comparator value recorded for 2025-10-26; if it ends up materially above 3.94, these probabilities would be somewhat too high.

I anchor on the FRED time-series analysis, which gives a combined P(increase by 2025-11-02) of 0.46 versus the late-October threshold. I shade that slightly down because the broader policy backdrop still points to balance-sheet runoff: ECB materials and Reuters indicate APP/PEPP holdings are still declining and the balance sheet decline is expected to remain gradual and predictable, while an October 2025 market forecast put the ECB balance sheet near €6.0tn by end-2025, below the current ~€6.195tn level. That makes late November less likely than early November to be above the 2025-10-26 baseline. For late January, I still keep the probability below 50% because QT/runoff remains the dominant structural force, but I move it a bit back toward 0.5 because forecast uncertainty is higher at that horizon and year-end balance-sheet/liquidity effects can create temporary reversals in weekly data.

Primary anchor is the FRED time-series output through 2025-10-24, which gives a combined estimate of 0.63 that OVX will be above 38.7 by 2025-11-02, with a modest positive short-term trend. I discount that somewhat because the true comparison value is the unknown 2025-10-26 level, not 10-24, and because OVX is a volatile, mean-reverting implied-vol index.
