By 2025-10-26, no qualifying broad post-May rule appears to have been implemented yet, despite strong January policy momentum. The biggest update is that BIS/Commerce officially moved in May to rescind and not enforce the January AI Diffusion Rule, while saying a replacement would come later; searches did not surface a concrete late-2025 timetable or any Federal Register/EAR action after May 1 implementing new broad controls on advanced AI chips, cloud access, or model weights. With only about two months left in 2025, a new interim final rule is still plausible, but the absence of a published rule or clear schedule makes NO more likely than YES.

I estimate a 9% chance. By late Oct. 2025, Congress had introduced several AI-specific bills with potentially qualifying provisions, but the ones that most clearly regulate AI use or development were still only at committee/introduction stage and had little time left to clear both chambers and be signed. The strongest broad AI-related provision on a must-pass vehicle was stripped from H.R.1 by a 99-1 Senate vote, which is a strong signal against late-session enactment of substantive AI regulation. The main live path appears to be the FY2026 NDAA, because the Senate passed AI-related chip provisions there, but that route is weak: those provisions still had to survive conference and may not satisfy the question’s requirement to regulate AI systems/models or their development rather than semiconductors/export priorities. Overall, the combination of a short remaining window, thin legislative momentum, and qualification uncertainty keeps the probability low but not negligible.

I put this at 10%. The coalition is real and increasingly organized: 26 countries reportedly pledged some form of presence, and a Paris headquarters has been established. But the decisive pattern in reporting through late October 2025 is that actual deployment—ground, air, or sea—is framed as a reassurance force for after a ceasefire/peace deal, not an immediate wartime intervention. Late-October Reuters reporting still showed diplomacy centered on ceasefire proposals, with Russia rejecting an immediate ceasefire on current lines. With barely two months left in 2025, the base case is no qualifying deployment. The main YES path is a sudden late-year ceasefire followed by a rapid symbolic coalition presence, or a narrow qualifying air/sea mission; that remains possible but clearly less likely than not.

Official UK and German government announcements from June 2022 already appear to satisfy the literal resolution criteria: the UK said it would gift M270 MLRS to Ukraine, and Germany said it would transfer M270/MARS launchers and GMLRS ammunition. M270/MARS are HIMARS-compatible systems explicitly contemplated by the question. So, unless the resolver unexpectedly excludes commitments made before the market opened, this looks effectively already resolved YES by the plain wording.

By late October 2025, the strongest quantitative picture points to Russian gas imports being well below 15 bcm per quarter. Reported totals show about 27.6 bcm of Russian gas imported by the EU in Jan-Sep 2025, or roughly 9.2 bcm per quarter on average, while TurkStream delivered only 13.8 bcm over the first three quarters. That makes a Q1-Q3 breach of 15 bcm very implausible, and leaves Q4 as the only realistic path. But reaching 15 bcm in Q4 would require a very large late-year jump in Russian LNG on top of fairly capped pipeline flows, and I have not found evidence of such a surge by the cutoff date.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the best late-October evidence points to Musk being back at #1 on Bloomberg with a substantial tens-of-billions lead over Larry Ellison, while all other rivals were far behind. Over only ~2 months, #1 tends to persist when the lead is that large. The main risk is that this ranking is very sensitive to Tesla/Oracle volatility and Bloomberg-specific methodology, as shown by Ellison’s brief September overtake, so this is well below certain.

Iran closing or severely restricting Hormuz by end-2025 remains a real tail risk because threats persisted into October, parliament backed the idea in June, and U.S. maritime advisories show a live seizure/boarding hazard. But the stronger signal is restraint: even during the hotter June Israel-Iran crisis, shipping monitors and vessel tracking said the strait stayed open and flows were stable, while reporting characterized the disruption as interference/caution rather than closure. With only about two months left from the forecast date, and with closure still highly costly for Iran itself and likely to trigger overwhelming response, I put the chance of a market-qualifying disruption at 19%.

Formal declarations of war are extremely rare in modern interstate disputes, and especially unlikely between nuclear-armed India and Pakistan. The strongest evidence is that during the worst India-Pakistan fighting in decades in May 2025—missiles, drones, fighter jets, and dozens of deaths—neither side formally declared war. Instead, they accepted a U.S.-brokered ceasefire, reinforced it through military hotlines, and moved toward troop reductions. That suggests that even under peak stress, both governments preferred limited strikes and deterrent signaling over the very specific act this market requires. There is still some residual risk from another major terrorist attack or sudden re-escalation before year-end, but with only about two months left from the cutoff and no strong evidence that a formal declaration was being prepared, I put the chance at the minimum 5%.

By 2025-10-26, the strongest pro-YES evidence is the spring reporting that Tesla directors explored succession options and that some investors were openly demanding a succession plan. But the more decision-relevant late-October evidence points the other way: Musk was still actively leading Tesla's Q3 earnings call, outlining company strategy into 2026, and Tesla's board was simultaneously backing an enormous new compensation package for him. That combination is much more consistent with continued tenure than with an imminent resignation or forced removal in the remaining ~2 months. So YES mainly comes from tail risks: an unforeseen legal/personal/health shock, or a surprise voluntary exit. Overall I put it at 11%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Tesla had clearly launched and expanded a real Austin robotaxi service, which keeps this from being a near-zero event. The fleet had grown into the ~20-30 vehicle range and public access had widened, so if qualifying rides began soon, 10,000 cumulative rides would be operationally achievable. But the market’s definition is much stricter than a normal robotaxi pilot: no in-car observer and no human backup/teleoperation. The strongest late evidence points against Tesla already being there. Initial launch rides had passenger-seat monitors; September coverage still described human monitors in-car; Forbes reported apparent remote human overrides; and on Oct. 22 Musk was still saying Tesla would remove safety monitors 'by the end of the year,' which implies they were not yet gone by the cutoff. Given Tesla’s history of optimistic autonomy timelines, I think the probability they clear both the autonomy-definition hurdle and the ride-volume hurdle by Dec. 31 is low, though not negligible because the scale hurdle is solvable if they do remove monitors soon.

I estimate a 79% chance of YES. By the cutoff, the key facts all pointed upward: South Korea’s official 2024 TFR had already risen to 0.75 from 0.72 in 2023, so 2025 only needed to stay above that old trough rather than improve dramatically (search_0_result_1). Marriages surged 14.9% in 2024, a strong leading indicator in Korea where births are overwhelmingly within marriage (search_0_result_1). Then 2025 flow data stayed strong: Q1 TFR was 0.82 vs 0.77 a year earlier, and births in Jan–May were up 6.9% y/y (search_1_summary). An official-linked forecast around 0.79 for 2025 reinforced that a result above 0.72 was more likely than not (search_3_result_3). The main reason not to go higher is that some of the rebound may reflect post-pandemic marriage catch-up, and Statistics Korea said it was still hard to know whether the increase would continue through the second half of 2025 (search_3_summary).

Agent submitted with p=0.180. Evidence for: ['Credible reports from Reuters and CNN indicate the board explored executive search firms and that Musk privately expressed a desire to step down.', 'The board disclosed that Musk conditionally threatened to pursue other interests if a new compensation deal providing control and assurances is not secured.', 'The upcoming Nov. 6 shareholder vote on a massive retention-focused pay package serves as a high-stakes catalyst for a potential exit if rejected.']. Evidence against: ['Tesla’s chair publicly denied the existence of a formal replacement search and reaffirmed the board’s full confidence in Musk’s leadership.', 'Recent board actions, specifically the proposal of a 10-year incentive package, demonstrate a proactive strategy to retain Musk rather than replace him.', 'Analysts and compensation experts anticipate the November pay package will pass, neutralizing the primary near-term trigger for his departure.', 'Recent reporting through October 2025 lacks any evidence of an imminent resignation and instead focuses on efforts to lock in his long-term tenure.'].

As of 2025-10-26, the strongest evidence is that the June 2025 US-Iran exchange was a short, contained episode: targeted U.S. strikes, symbolic Iranian retaliation, then a ceasefire within about a day and broad reporting that the 'air war' had ended. That seems below this market’s bar for a YES, which requires a sustained, publicly acknowledged war-like conflict. By late October, major reporting was centered on sanctions, economic pressure, diplomacy, and regional crisis management rather than active US-Iran war. There is still some residual risk because the U.S. already crossed the threshold to direct strikes once in 2025 and nuclear talks had broken down, so a new trigger could emerge before year-end. But with only about two months left and no evidence of renewed sustained direct conflict by the cutoff, I put the chance at 8%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, multiple pre-cutoff sources indicate San Diego FC had already qualified for the MLS playoffs and in fact finished as the No. 1 seed in the Western Conference. Full-result summaries state SDFC finished first in the West with 63 points and had a postseason game scheduled for Oct. 26 (search_0_summary). Since the qualifying event had effectively already occurred by the cutoff, this should resolve YES absent an unexpected interpretation issue.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the only credible near-term human lunar landing program was Artemis, and NASA's own 2025 materials had Artemis II at April 2026 and Artemis III at mid-2027. Artemis II is a prerequisite for Artemis III, so a 2025 landing was effectively off the table. Searches also found no alternative crewed lunar attempt in 2025: China was still targeting 2030, private lunar missions in 2025 were robotic, and late-2025 reporting said Starship HLS delays were severe enough to threaten even the 2027 Artemis III target. The remaining YES paths were essentially surprise/hidden missions, so I stay at the minimum allowed probability.

I estimate a 24% chance that TSLA trades below $200 at some point before 2025-12-31. By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the best available evidence suggests it had not yet gone below $200 in 2025: sources place the spring low around $222 and a 52-week low at $202.59, i.e. close but not through the threshold. More importantly, late-September to late-October reporting indicates TSLA had rebounded strongly and was likely trading in the high $300s to low $400s, even after an October post-earnings drop. From that level, a sub-$200 print in the remaining ~2 months would require an additional roughly 35% to 55% crash. Tesla is volatile and faces real risks (profit misses, margin pressure, tax-credit expiry demand cliff), so the probability is not tiny. But absent evidence of an acute late-October breakdown or already-breached threshold, such a collapse by year-end is still less likely than not.

Trump had already won by the forecast date, so the question effectively reduces to whether the relevant BLS headline CPI reading available by 2025-12-10 will be below 2.5%. As of 2025-10-26, the latest official print was September CPI at 3.0% y/y, up from 2.9% in August, and core CPI was also 3.0% y/y. Professional previews/nowcasts for the next print were still around 3.0%-3.1% y/y, with monthly inflation around 0.3%-0.4%, not the kind of softness usually needed to get under 2.5% within one or two releases. The main thing keeping this above a very low probability is that some of the recent firmness came from gasoline/energy and there is ambiguity about whether the resolving print is October or November CPI; favorable base effects plus a soft November could still do it. But given the starting point and consensus forecasts, sub-2.5% by Dec. 10 looks unlikely.

Because Trump's Nobel outcome is already NO, this market is effectively asking whether there will be no Ukraine ceasefire before 2026. As of the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, the strongest evidence points against a year-end ceasefire: Russia had just rejected an immediate ceasefire, kept its terms unchanged, and the hoped-for Trump-Putin summit was paused/stalled, while Trump also shifted away from demanding an immediate pause and imposed new sanctions. I still leave roughly a one-in-five to one-in-four chance of a ceasefire because high-level diplomacy and summit preparations were not fully dead, but the balance of evidence favors no ceasefire.

By 2025-10-26, I find no direct evidence that SNL had already done a 2025 Waymo sketch, and the remaining opportunity set looked limited: the fall schedule had only a few confirmed new episodes left in 2025 by then (Nov. 1, Nov. 8, Nov. 15), with any December shows still uncertain (search_2_result_0; search_2_summary). Waymo was definitely culturally visible enough to be sketch material—250,000+ paid rides per week, the LA robotaxi fires, and the October San Francisco prank all gave it comic/news value (search_3_summary). But the best targeted SNL/Waymo search I found surfaced only an old 2021 Weekend Update joke, which suggests SNL has historically treated Waymo more as occasional joke fodder than as the centerpiece of a sketch (search_5_result_0). Net: plausible but still clearly unlikely.

Musk’s July 2025 announcement of the “America Party” makes a YES outcome plausible, especially because the resolution criteria are fairly broad and don’t require ballot access or actual candidates yet. But the contemporaneous reporting was full of caveats: AP and others said it was unclear whether anything had actually been established; the visible X account was community-run and disclaimed official affiliation; fake or dubious FEC filings circulated; and Reuters later reported on Aug. 20 that Musk was quietly putting the brakes on the effort to focus on his companies. By the Oct. 26 cutoff I found no strong authoritative evidence of renewed momentum or official party infrastructure. So the most likely path is NO, with some residual chance Musk reverses course and launches minimal qualifying infrastructure before Dec. 31.

By 2025-10-26, Iran looked stressed but still far from losing control of Tehran within the remaining two months of 2025. The strongest pro-YES factors are war damage, sanctions, economic deterioration, Khamenei health/succession concerns, and some elite factionalism. But the more decisive evidence points the other way: Reuters reported no significant anti-regime protests even after the 12-day war, the regime was carrying out effective crackdowns and external security coordination, and mainstream expert assessments described the system as rattled yet resilient, with continuity mechanisms for succession and coups seen as unlikely. Since ordinary succession inside the system would resolve NO, and because the window to year-end is short, I put the chance of actual regime loss of control in Tehran in 2025 at 6%.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the evidence strongly indicates hallucinations remain an outstanding problem, not a solved one. Multiple 2025 surveys and reviews still describe hallucination as a critical unresolved barrier. More importantly, concrete late-2025 evidence shows frontier models still hallucinate in consequential ways: OpenAI's own reported benchmark results said newer reasoning models hallucinated more, not less; independent testing found fabricated claims of tool use/actions; and legal/business incidents involving fake citations and fabricated references continued through October 2025. The best counterpoint is that grounding/RAG/citation-based systems have improved substantially, with benchmark scores like 83.6% on DeepMind's FACTS Grounding leaderboard for top models in constrained settings. But that looks like mitigation, not a full fix—especially since even those sources describe factuality as still imperfect, and grounded systems themselves can still misread or misuse retrieved evidence. Given the subjective resolution standard ('fixed' vs. 'outstanding problem still exists'), and only about two months remaining after the cutoff, a YES would require a very rapid and broadly recognized shift that seems unlikely. I therefore put YES at 7%.

Most civil cases do settle eventually, but this question is specifically about settlement by the end of 2025, with only about two months left from the Oct. 26 cutoff. The strongest concrete evidence I found is that this federal case was still actively contested in September 2025, with the court ruling on discovery/protective-order disputes rather than recording any dismissal or settlement signal. I also did not find evidence of a near-term mediation or trial catalyst before year-end. That pushes me below 50%, though not dramatically, because civil litigants can still settle at any time and counsel were at least communicating in the ordinary course of litigation.

Nigeria almost certainly did not reach 95% adult account ownership before 2025. The outside view was already bleak: jumping from about 40% account ownership in 2017 to 95% by 2024 would require an extraordinary acceleration. The inside-view evidence is much worse for YES. EFInA-linked 2023 reporting put formal financial inclusion at only 64% and headline inclusion at 74%, with tens of millions still excluded (search_2_result_0, search_2_result_8). CGAP reported only 45% of adults had a formal bank account in early 2023 (search_0_result_7). Most decisively, a 2025 article summarizing Global Findex 2025 gave Nigeria’s 2024 account ownership as 63.3% (search_3_result_5), which is directly relevant and nowhere near 95%. Even allowing for definitional noise and some late improvement from mobile money, the gap is far too large.

I lean NO. The only plausible path to YES is that the CBD’s long-standing ‘de facto’ geoengineering moratorium is judged to count as a treaty obligation for many parties. But the strongest legal evidence I found says COP decisions are generally not legally binding when they create new obligations, and multiple sources still describe solar radiation management as legal in most nations. I found political momentum and ministerial opposition in 2025, but no clear new treaty/protocol with 10+ state parties before the cutoff.

The resolution bar is extraordinarily high and, by the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the published record appears far short of it. There is real progress: a peer-reviewed Chemical Geology paper exists, the spherules show unusual chemistry, and multiple institutions are involved. But the strongest positive evidence only supports 'unusual' or possibly extrasolar/nonstandard material, not a 95%-confidence technological origin. Meanwhile, a 2025 peer-reviewed re-evaluation argues many spherules are likely volcanic/terrestrial, attacking the chain of inference before one even gets to alien technology. The literature still seems to be debating terrestrial-natural vs interstellar-natural explanations, while some project sources were still planning a 2025 follow-up expedition to look for larger fragments. Given the lack of any clear late-2025 paper explicitly concluding both non-Earth and technological origin with the required confidence, I put this at the floor-ish end of plausible probabilities: 5%.

By 2025-10-26, the preponderance of evidence suggests this had not become a broad multi-stock bust. The Magnificent Seven were still described in late October as having 'impressed' and as being on a tear overall, with Reuters arguing the AI bubble was not near a peak (search_1_result_3, search_1_result_9). The strongest direct stress evidence is Tesla, which had nearly halved after its December 2024 record and may have crossed the 50%-below-ATH threshold (search_4_summary). But the rest of the cohort appears far short: the group drawdown in early 2025 was only about 14.23%, Tesla was the worst YTD loser at roughly one-third to 38% down, and several names posted sharp rebounds in April 2025 (search_1_summary, search_5_summary). So YES seems to require either undiscovered pre-cutoff threshold breaches by multiple names or an extreme final-two-month crash in several mega-cap stocks. That is possible, but unlikely.

The most recent clearly surfaced CDC/public discussion still puts annual U.S. AMR deaths around 35,000-37,000, far below the 70,000 threshold. That makes a simple organic doubling by end-2025 unlikely. The main path to YES is methodological: a credible source could publish a broader U.S. estimate, especially an 'associated with AMR' figure rather than a narrower directly attributable count. There is some evidence CDC planned additional 2025 threat estimates, so a late-2025 official update is possible. But by the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff I did not find a clear U.S.-specific credible estimate already at or above 70,000, and only a short window remained before resolution. So YES is plausible but still the minority case.

By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, the key issue is no longer regional availability but whether GTA VI launches at all in 2025. Multiple major outlets reported an official delay on May 2, 2025 from fall 2025 to May 26, 2026, including Reuters, CNN, and The Verge (search_1_result_0, search_1_result_2, search_1_result_7). Later pre-cutoff reporting and Take-Two investor materials continued to cite a 2026 launch, including an August 2025 Take-Two deck saying 'GTA VI launching May 26, 2026' and Reuters in June saying it was scheduled for 2026 (search_3_result_3, search_3_result_4). Since Europe would almost certainly be included in any normal launch, a YES outcome now requires an extraordinary reversal and sale in Europe before year-end 2025, which looks very unlikely.

Bitcoin looked more likely than not to finish 2025 above its Jan. 1 close of $93,429.20. By early October 2025 Reuters reported BTC at a new all-time high around $125,245, roughly 34% above the threshold, and even after a mid-October risk-off selloff it was still about $113,129, around 21% above the threshold. That means a NO resolution would require a fairly substantial further drawdown over the remaining ~2.5 months. Supportive context also matters: Reuters described strong investor demand, record crypto ETF inflows, regulatory wins, and Fed-easing tailwinds during 2025. The main reason not to go higher than ~80% is Bitcoin’s well-demonstrated downside volatility: Reuters also described a sharp October drop and record liquidations/freefall risk, so a 15-20%+ late-year decline was very plausible. Netting those factors out, YES is favored but far from certain.

By 2025-10-26, Erin itself had only reached 160 mph, and available reports indicate Humberto also peaked at 160 mph rather than above it, so the question had likely not already resolved YES. The remaining path to YES required a new Atlantic hurricane in the final ~5 weeks of the season to exceed 160 mph — an extremely rare late-season outcome. That risk was reduced further by late-October reporting that the season was entering its tail end and, most importantly, by an Oct. 26 Atlantic outlook saying tropical cyclone formation was not expected during the next 7 days. I therefore put the chance in the low single digits, but not near zero because rare November western Caribbean explosions can happen.

Chegg looks genuinely distressed, but a bankruptcy filing before Jan. 1, 2026 still seems unlikely on this very short horizon. The strongest evidence against an immediate filing is that Chegg repaid its March 2025 convert maturity, kept buying back 2026 notes at discounts, ended Q1 with meaningful cash/investments, and reported positive adjusted EBITDA in Q2 rather than a clear near-term liquidity collapse. The main case for YES is rapid AI-driven revenue erosion and ongoing restructuring, which could make 2026 dangerous; but based on the evidence available by Oct. 26, 2025, that risk appears more medium-term than likely to crystallize in the next two months.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the case for a YES resolution looked very weak. The original flagship RK anomaly had already been substantially undone by LHCb’s 2022 revision, which brought RK and RK* back into consistency with the Standard Model (lookup_0). Review-style sources from 2025 still described the strongest remaining global LFU tension as only about 3.1σ, far short of the 5σ discovery threshold required for resolution (search_1_summary). Recent Belle II peer-reviewed measurements in 2024-2025 were described as consistent with the Standard Model or with world averages, not as converging toward a discovery claim (search_4_summary). I also found no evidence of any peer-reviewed 2024-2025 paper already claiming >=5σ LFU violation, and with only ~2 months remaining after the cutoff, the time available for a brand-new result to clear both 5σ and peer review was very short. A small residual probability remains for an unexpected late-2025 paper in a less-publicized channel or a fast-moving accepted result, but overall NO was overwhelmingly more likely.

I estimate Mikie Sherrill has about a 71% chance to win. By late October 2025, the best available evidence shows her as the modest but clear favorite: several late polls have her ahead by 5-7 points and often around 50%, while Democrats retain a very large registration advantage and Sherrill has a cash edge. But this is not a lock. One late poll showed a dead heat, multiple reports say the race has tightened, and Ciattarelli appears to have stronger enthusiasm/turnout potential in an off-year contest where New Jersey Republicans can be competitive. Net: Sherrill is favored, but a GOP upset remains quite plausible.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the best available evidence already indicated FY2025 customs duties were about $195b, not anywhere near $500b. Both the CBO/reporting summaries and secondary analysis of Treasury’s final Monthly Treasury Statement put FY2025 customs duties at roughly $195b (search_1_summary; search_0_result_3). That is an enormous increase from prior years, but still less than half the lower bound needed for YES. To resolve YES, the eventual FY2025 Financial Report would have to differ from these near-final figures by more than $300b, which is far beyond normal reporting revisions. The only pro-YES point I found was commentary about a possible '$500b annual pace' later on, but that refers to a run rate, not actual FY2025 collections (search_1_summary). So the chance that the audited FY2025 Financial Report lands between $500b and $1t looks extremely low.

Anthropic looks materially more likely than the base rate because late-October secondary reports say Claude Opus 4.1 is already #1 on Chatbot Arena and Claude Sonnet 4.5 is #2, with GPT-5 far behind; the alphabetical tiebreak also slightly helps Anthropic. But I’m keeping this below 50% because that ranking evidence is indirect rather than from the official leaderboard, and there is meaningful risk that Google launches Gemini 3 before year-end and overtakes in the remaining two months.

As of 2025-10-26, Kaiser appears to be a real but clearly second-tier contender. Multiple late-October polls put him only around 11-13%, while Jeannette Jara and José Antonio Kast are both materially ahead and Matthei is usually ahead too. With only about three weeks left, a jump from fourth/fifth place into first would require an unusually large late break or a major rival collapse, and I found no strong evidence of such a shock before the cutoff. So his chance to top the first round looks low but not impossible.

Brooklyn looked like one of the NBA’s weakest teams at the 2025-10-26 cutoff: preseason markets had them at roughly 19.5 wins and +50000 to win the East, while playoff odds were only +4000 and championship odds +100000. Multiple previews described the Nets as an intentional rebuild focused on development, with an unusually young roster, five first-round rookies, and even the possibility of moving veterans later in the season. The East still had many far more plausible contenders—Cleveland, New York, Orlando, Atlanta, Detroit, Boston, Philadelphia, Milwaukee, and Indiana—even after some star injuries. Brooklyn also opened the season with a 19-point loss to Charlotte, which offered no early signal that the market had badly underrated them. My true estimate would be below 5%, but under the instruction-imposed floor, I submit 5%.

Moonshot is a real contender, not a fringe one: Kimi K2/K2-0905 appears to have reached the LM Arena top tier in 2025. But the available evidence before the cutoff points to Moonshot being in the top 10 rather than at or near #1, with other labs still seen as the main leaders. Given only a few days from the forecast date to resolution, I think Moonshot’s chance of ending up on top is low but nontrivial.

Section 4 removal is unprecedented, requires Vance plus a majority of a loyal Cabinet, and if Trump contests it would then require two-thirds in both chambers of a Republican-controlled Congress. As of 2025-10-26, the evidence points to public chatter and opposition calls, not real internal movement. The main plausible path to YES would be a sudden incapacitating medical event in the remaining two months, but the latest authoritative reporting instead says Trump is active and officially in 'exceptional health.' Given the platform floor, I go with 5%.

I put this at the floor, 5%. By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, the best evidence points to Russia treating the Finnish border as a longer-term militarized front, not as a near-term invasion target before year-end. There is some genuine cause for concern: new infrastructure is being built near the border, Finland’s intelligence leadership warned Russia’s force reforms threaten NATO, and Finnish security services still view Russia as the main long-run threat. But the more decision-relevant, late-date evidence is strongly against a 2025 attack: NATO officials said the observed buildup was nothing like the pre-2022 Ukraine invasion posture; Finnish officials described it as minor; reports said Russia had very few troops on the frontier because of Ukraine; PM Orpo said there was no immediate threat; and as late as Oct. 9, President Stubb said Russia was not an imminent military threat to Finland. Meanwhile, the hostile actions actually reported in 2025 were hybrid pressure tactics like migration weaponization and GPS jamming, not territorial seizure. A Russian move into Finland in 2025 would mean attacking NATO territory and risking a massive escalation for little clear gain, with only about two months left in the resolution window. The residual 5% is for miscalculation, a limited grey-zone land grab, or a sudden shock not visible in public reporting.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the strongest fact is that headline CPI was only 3.0% year-over-year in September 2025, up just 0.1 point from August, with only the October, November, and December 2025 releases left that could trigger YES. That leaves inflation 1.1 points short of the 4.1% threshold. Recent monthly inflation was moderate rather than explosive, and commentary around late 2025 emphasized tariff-related goods pressure being partly offset by services/shelter disinflation. The remaining path to 4.1% therefore appears to require an unusually sharp late-year burst, likely driven by a major energy or tariff shock. That is possible, but it looks clearly less likely than not. I put the chance at 7%.

As of 2025-10-26, the best anchor is the late-October betting market plus the Chiefs’ recent form. Kansas City had stumbled to 2-3, with injuries/suspensions at WR, but then rebounded with convincing wins over Detroit and Las Vegas, got Rashee Rice back, and by Oct. 20 was reportedly the new Super Bowl favorite at +500. That implies roughly a mid-teens title chance, which fits an elite Mahomes/Reid team that is dangerous but far from dominant at 4-3.

Matt Salsberg was confirmed in the 64-player bracket, but this is a high-variance single-elimination event where an average entrant starts near 1.6%. More importantly, by the Oct. 26 cutoff the tournament had already been completed in August, and multiple pre-cutoff reports said leaked information and betting activity pointed strongly to Sam Soverel as the winner, with Soverel trading above 90% and reportedly up to 99.4%. No comparable evidence points to Salsberg, so I put him at the floor 5%.

Avatar: Fire and Ash looks like a real Best Picture contender by late October 2025, but not a leading winner. Multiple awards outlets include it in projected Best Picture lineups or frontrunner lists, and the franchise has strong nomination precedent because both earlier Avatar films made Best Picture (search_0_result_4, search_0_result_6, search_1_result_2). That said, major trades still classify it as 'Time Will Tell' or 'Still to See/Under Embargo,' meaning no critic/voter-facing validation yet, and coverage of comparative odds points to other films like Sinners and Sentimental Value as more established favorites (search_0_result_5, search_1_result_2, search_3_summary). Trailer/footage reactions suggest a darker, more emotional film than prior entries, which helps a bit, but this still looks more like a plausible nominee with strong craft upside than the most likely Best Picture winner (search_4_result_0, search_4_result_3, search_4_result_8).

Eminem looks like a long shot. Spotify’s 2024 Wrapped baseline had him only around #12 overall and behind multiple likely 2025 contenders, while Taylor Swift was #1 (search_0_result_0; search_0_result_1; search_0_result_4). Through 2025, the available evidence points to only modest catalysts for Eminem—a viral catalog bump and a small September soundtrack/single release, with no major official 2025 tour announced—rather than the kind of blockbuster year that usually produces Spotify’s global #1 artist (search_3_result_4; search_3_result_6; search_3_result_3). Ranking-style 2025 sources also point to Swift/Bad Bunny/Drake/The Weeknd-tier acts remaining ahead, with at least one source explicitly noting Eminem slipping in rank (search_2_result_4; search_2_result_7). Because probabilities must stay within 0.05-0.95, I submit 5% rather than lower.

By Oct. 26, 2025, Article 5 still looked very unlikely to be invoked by year-end. The base rate is extremely low: NATO’s own materials describe Article 5 as having been invoked only once in its history (after 9/11) (search_1_result_0). More importantly, the strongest 2025 near-miss we found — Poland’s September drone incursions — led to Article 4 consultations, not Article 5 (search_2_result_1). Reuters also reported in late October that NATO’s top military commander thought Russia had been deterred and was being more careful after NATO’s response (search_3_result_0). There was clearly elevated risk from repeated hybrid and border incidents, so I don’t go below 5%, but absent a clearly attributable armed attack on NATO territory, the chance of formal Article 5 invocation in the remaining weeks of 2025 looked low.

Billie Eilish is clearly a plausible top-tier contender, but as of 2025-10-26 she looked more like a strong top-5 artist than the most likely #1. The official 2024 baseline had her only fifth globally, behind Taylor Swift, The Weeknd, Bad Bunny, and Drake, even though she had the No. 2 album and a huge song in 'Birds of a Feather' (search_0_result_3) (search_0_result_2). That song’s durability keeps her in the race, since it was reported as Spotify’s most-streamed song of 2024 (search_4_result_1) (search_4_result_5). But the stronger 2025-specific evidence points away from her: searches did not surface a comparable major Billie 2025 release, while Billboard only had her teasing new music and more tour dates (search_4_result_0), and Bad Bunny launched a massive 2025 album cycle with record-level Spotify debut numbers and a huge streaming week (search_3_result_4) (search_3_result_6). Given the concentrated winner pool and the lack of late-October evidence that Billie was leading year-to-date, I put her chances at about 7%.

OpenAI looked like one of the few organizations that might plausibly make an AGI claim in 2025: Altman said early in the year that the company was confident it knew how to build AGI and suggested 2025 would bring major agentic progress. But by late October 2025, the stronger evidence points against an explicit year-end announcement. Altman increasingly described AGI as a 'sloppy' or even 'pointless' term and preferred talking about levels of progress, which directly reduces the chance of a crisp 'we achieved AGI' statement. More importantly, mid-2025 reporting indicated that an AGI declaration could trigger major Microsoft/OpenAI contract consequences, giving OpenAI a concrete incentive to avoid formal use of the term. Finally, OpenAI had already unveiled GPT-5 and held DevDay 2025 without making such an announcement, despite those being natural moments to do so. With only about two months left as of the cutoff, I put the chance of an official AGI announcement in 2025 at 12%.

As of 2025-10-26, Monad had moved well beyond rumor stage: it had officially named the MON token, opened an airdrop eligibility/claim portal, and credible outlets were describing this as ahead of token launch. Multiple pre-market venues were already supporting MON trading, which is a meaningful sign that a real TGE was expected soon. The main reason this is not higher is that authoritative reporting still said the actual airdrop, tokenomics, and mainnet launch would come at a later date, with no firm public date yet. Since resolution requires not just a portal but an actual token launch plus a qualifying airdrop by Nov. 30, the remaining schedule risk is substantial. Net: more likely than not, but far from certain.

By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, the outside view is already very unfavorable: WHO pandemic declarations for novel coronaviruses are exceptionally rare, and only about two months remain until resolution. The specific trigger for this market, HKU5-CoV-2, appears still to be a laboratory/animal-risk story rather than a human outbreak: multiple 2025 reports say there were no confirmed human infections and that it entered human cells less readily than SARS-CoV-2. WHO searches also turned up continued monitoring/planning for SARS-CoV-2 and MERS, but no obvious 2025 WHO outbreak notice, emergency process, or pandemic-style signaling for a separate novel coronavirus. Given the lack of visible human spread by late October, a WHO declaration of a new pandemic by Dec. 31 would require an abrupt and severe late-year development that leaves little trace in pre-cutoff reporting. That can’t be ruled out entirely, but it looks very unlikely.

I estimate a 28% chance that NASA GISTEMP will report October 2025 below 1.10°C above the 1951-1980 baseline. The strongest direct evidence I found is that October 2024 was reportedly 1.32°C in GISTEMP, so a YES outcome would require a drop of more than 0.22°C year over year. Meanwhile, independent Copernicus data show September 2025 was still the third-warmest September on record and only about 0.07°C cooler than September 2024, which suggests 2025 remains extremely warm even if a bit below 2024. ENSO-neutral conditions do provide some cooling relative to the 2023-24 El Niño peak, so YES is far from impossible, but the threshold looks meaningfully below the most likely October 2025 outcome.

As of 2025-10-26, the Chiefs were chasing rather than leading the AFC West. Denver had improved to 6-2 with a Week 8 blowout of Dallas, while Kansas City was still 4-3 and had already lost the head-to-head tiebreaker to the Chargers from Week 1. That makes KC's path clearly harder than an even-money prior. I do not go much lower because the Chiefs still had a game in hand on Denver, had been the preseason/early-season market favorite, and their Mahomes/Reid track record means they remain very live over the back half. Net: plausible, but more likely NO than YES.

By Oct. 26, 2025, September had already delivered the first required cut, but the exact sequence then hinged on an October pause. That looked very unlikely: Reuters reported near-100% futures certainty of an October cut and a 115-of-117 economist consensus for a cut on Oct. 29. December easing was also widely expected, so the main bottleneck was October. Given the overwhelming pre-meeting expectation of cut-cut rather than cut-pause-cut, I put YES at 6%.

Moonshot is a real contender, not a fringe lab: Kimi K2 was widely reported as frontier-competitive, and Moonshot shipped a follow-on K2-0905 update with improved reasoning/coding. But the strongest pre-cutoff evidence I found still places Moonshot well short of the lead—top 10, and specifically tied for 8th in September—while rivals like OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, xAI, and others remain very active. With only about two months from the cutoff to resolution, Moonshot likely needs another major jump, so I see them as a live but clear underdog.

A narrow 0.05°C NASA GISTEMP bracket would normally be a modest-probability outcome. The best pre-cutoff evidence I found suggests October 2025 is somewhat more likely to land above 1.14°C than inside 1.10-1.14°C: September 2025 was still reported by Copernicus as the third-warmest September on record and nearly as warm as September 2024, implying the background temperature regime remained very elevated, while ENSO-neutral / possible weak La Niña provides only a modest cooling counterforce. Without direct NASA September/October 2025 values before cutoff, there is meaningful mapping uncertainty from Copernicus to GISTEMP, so I do not go too low. Overall, the target band looks possible but a bit below the most likely October 2025 NASA outcome.

By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, the evidence points overwhelmingly to an exact 25 bp cut at the Oct. 28-29 FOMC meeting. The strongest signals are highly recent and specific: Reuters on Oct. 3 said CME FedWatch implied a 98% chance of a 25 bp October cut; InvestmentNews on Oct. 2 said FedWatch showed 99% odds of 25 bp and effectively no hold chance; and Reuters on Oct. 24 said futures reflected near-100% certainty of a 25 bp cut after softer-than-expected inflation. Reuters' Oct. 21 economist poll was even more striking: 115 of 117 economists expected a quarter-point cut on Oct. 29. Fed communication also leaned in that direction, with Governor Waller explicitly favoring another October cut because of labor-market worries. Against that, there was some caution from Barr and Schmid, plus elevated inflation and a small residual risk of either a pause or a 50 bp move. But given how close the meeting was, how strong the consensus was, and how central banks usually avoid surprising markets at that horizon, the chance of an exact 25 bp cut appears very high, though not certain.

Late-October evidence makes Mamdani the clear frontrunner, not the likeliest second-place finisher. Multiple independent polls had him ahead by double digits: Quinnipiac/NYT/ABC7 around 46-33 over Cuomo with Sliwa at 15 (search_1_result_5; search_1_result_6; search_1_result_8), AARP/Gotham around 43.2-28.9 with Sliwa 19.4 (search_0_result_2; search_0_result_5), and amNewYork/Fox around 52-28-14 among likely voters (search_1_result_1). The main path to YES is a late Cuomo surge that knocks Mamdani from first to second; that path exists because two-way scenarios are much tighter and Cuomo improved after Adams exited. But Sliwa publicly refused to drop out, keeping anti-Mamdani votes split (search_2_result_2), and late coverage still described Mamdani as the frontrunner facing Cuomo’s last-ditch effort (search_2_result_0). So YES is meaningfully possible but clearly not the modal outcome.

Le Havre look like an extreme long shot. The outside view is brutal: clubs of Le Havre’s size/profile almost never win Ligue 1 in the PSG era. Preseason coverage framed them as a survival/relegation-battle side, while PSG were the overwhelming favorite and Le Havre were absent from the contender tier. By the cutoff, official coverage still described Le Havre’s aim as survival, they had taken a 6-2 loss to Marseille, and even after a 1-0 win at Auxerre on Oct. 26 they were only 14th with 9 points after 9 matches—nowhere near title pace. I therefore keep them at the minimum allowed probability.

Walter Reeves appears to be a long-shot challenger to incumbent Andre Dickens, and the strongest late evidence is negative: multiple late-August/October candidate-list summaries and official Atlanta qualifying-page summaries do not list Reeves among the qualified mayoral candidates. Even if he did qualify, Dickens had major incumbent, fundraising, and name-recognition advantages. I leave a small residual probability for incomplete reporting or write-in/ballot-list uncertainty, but YES looks very unlikely.

xAI looks like a real but clearly secondary contender as of the cutoff. The best cutoff-relevant evidence I found is negative for this exact market: Grok-4 was only third on the text leaderboard in mid-July behind Google and OpenAI (search_0_result_8), and xAI’s own Sept. 19 announcement says grok-4-fast is only #8 on the relevant LMArena Text Arena and roughly on par with grok-4-0709 (search_3_result_1). xAI’s late-2025 roadmap also emphasized coding, multimodal, and video products rather than a clearly imminent new flagship text model before Dec. 31 (search_3_result_2). On top of that, xAI loses most ties for first on alphabetical order. Offsetting this, xAI has shown it can be competitive—its own Feb. 2025 post says an early Grok-3 version topped LMArena (search_0_result_6)—and frontier leaderboard leadership can change quickly. Still, with only about two months left from the cutoff, multiple strong rivals, and no strong evidence xAI was already at or near #1 on the text leaderboard by late October, I put xAI below 1-in-5.

By Oct. 26, 2025, the September leg of the pattern was already in place: the Fed had cut 25 bps to 4.00%-4.25%. But the decisive fact is that virtually every high-quality pre-meeting signal for the October 28-29 meeting pointed to another cut, not a pause. Reuters reported markets pricing an October cut at 98% on Oct. 3 and near-100% by Oct. 24, with 115 of 117 economists expecting a cut and major brokerages aligned. Reuters also said the Fed was still poised to cut despite the government shutdown and data vacuum. Since YES requires October to be a pause, this makes the target sequence a low-probability upset. Even though a hypothetical October pause could make a December pause somewhat more plausible, the October hurdle is so unfavorable that the overall YES probability is driven down to the floor of the allowed range.

I estimate an 11% chance of YES. The core reason is that by the Oct. 26 cutoff there is strong evidence of a serious US military buildup and repeated US kinetic strikes near Venezuela, but the best authoritative reporting available in these searches characterizes those strikes as targeting alleged drug-smuggling/cartel vessels rather than Venezuelan military forces. I found brinkmanship and proximity risk — e.g. Venezuelan F-16s buzzing a US destroyer and a much larger-than-normal US deployment — but no confirmed exchange of fire with FANB/coast-guard units and no confirmed US strike impacting Venezuelan land territory. With only about five days left in the operative event window, that keeps the probability in the low teens rather than higher.

The U.S.-Venezuela situation had clearly escalated by Oct. 26, 2025: sustained maritime strikes, an unusual Caribbean buildup, CIA authorization, and Trump openly talking about possible strikes on land. That makes the risk much higher than the normal base rate for a U.S. territorial invasion. But the specific market threshold is demanding: a military offensive intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory. The best available reporting still points away from that threshold. Multiple authoritative summaries say current force levels are insufficient for a full-scale invasion, SouthCom is framing operations as counternarcotics/readiness, experts say a full-scale invasion is unlikely, and as of late October no territorial-control offensive had begun. The most plausible escalations appear to be land strikes, special operations, or continued coercive maritime/air action, which may not satisfy resolution. With about two months remaining, I assign a low but nontrivial 8% chance of a qualifying invasion beginning by Dec. 31, 2025.

My forecast is dominated by evidence that the ceasefire may already have been broken shortly after it was announced. While the outside-view chance of a several-month-old ceasefire failing within the next 5 days would only be around the low 20s, a credible pre-cutoff report says Israel said Iran fired missiles after the ceasefire took effect and that Israel retaliated by striking a radar system near Tehran, which would satisfy this market if acknowledged/consensus-reported (search_1_result_2). Reuters-derived June 25 reporting also indicates continued strike activity on Iranian sites in the immediate ceasefire period (search_3_summary). The main residual uncertainty is technical: whether those actions were clearly after the ceasefire took effect and qualify under the market’s exact impact/acknowledgment criteria. Because of that ambiguity I stop short of certainty, but the event looks much more likely than not to have already resolved YES by the Oct. 26 forecast date.

I estimate a 5% chance. The key distinction is that the market resolves YES only for a military offensive intended to establish control over Iranian territory, not for airstrikes, naval clashes, or punitive attacks. By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, the evidence shows the U.S. had already carried out direct strikes on Iranian nuclear sites, so escalation risk is real. But the same reporting consistently framed U.S. objectives as limited: Defense Secretary Hegseth said the June operation was not about regime change, Reuters reported statements that there would not be American boots on the ground in Iran, and Reuters coverage of force posture described added defensive capabilities and fighter aircraft rather than the kind of large-scale ground logistics an invasion would require. The short remaining horizon to Dec. 31 also matters: a territorial offensive against Iran would be a major war decision that is historically rare and usually preceded by unmistakable troop buildup and political signaling. I found no credible pre-cutoff reporting of actual U.S. seizure of Iranian territory or concrete plans to do so. So while further strikes or a Hormuz crisis remained plausible, a true invasion/occupation looked very unlikely.

As of 2025-10-26, The Weeknd looks like a real contender but not the likeliest winner. The bullish case is strong: he released a major 22-track album at the end of January 2025, giving him almost a full year of extra catalog consumption, and that album opened huge on Spotify. He also appears to have maintained exceptional reach by late 2025 via very high monthly-listener rankings. But the bearish case is stronger: Taylor Swift was the official 2024 Wrapped winner for a second straight year, and I have not found authoritative late-October evidence that The Weeknd is actually leading 2025 cumulative artist streams—the metric Spotify Wrapped uses. With Taylor, Bad Bunny, and possibly Bruno/Drake also in the mix, The Weeknd seems more like one of several plausible challengers than the favorite.

Polymarket looked materially closer than a typical regulated-launch case by the cutoff: it had acquired QCEX/QCX, received CFTC no-action/approval relief, and filed contracts that could list no earlier than Oct. 2. But the best late-October reporting still treated the U.S. relaunch as upcoming rather than live, and the federal shutdown/CFTC disruption appeared to be an active drag into the final week before the cutoff. So I make YES a slight favorite, but only narrowly.

By the Oct. 26, 2025 forecast date, the strongest evidence points to a bounded air war rather than an impending US ground incursion into Iran. Reporting on the June 2025 US attack describes air/sea operations against Iranian nuclear sites, not terrestrial entry by regular US troops (search_0_summary). Senior US officials framed the strikes as 'surgical,' explicitly avoiding 'boots on the ground,' and still spoke in terms of deterrence and deal-making rather than invasion or regime-change war (search_1_summary). Even after Iran directly retaliated against a US base in Qatar, the response appears to have been interpreted as calibrated and was followed quickly by a ceasefire push rather than a spiral into broader war (search_3_summary). By late October, the regional picture in the available reporting is one of ceasefire management, proxy activity, and regional basing—not momentum toward regular US ground combat on Iranian soil (search_2_summary). The main remaining path to YES is a tail-risk contingency: a sudden severe attack on US forces, a rescue/recovery mission, or another crisis forcing a limited incursion. But because the market excludes SOF/advisors and requires active regular personnel physically entering Iran for combat, the bar for YES is unusually high. With only about two months left in the resolution window, I put the chance at 6%.

Minecraft already posted a finalized ~$163M 3-day domestic opening, which is a very high bar. By 2025-10-26, none of the major releases that had opened had beaten it: Lilo & Stitch was at $146M for the 3-day Memorial Day opening, Superman at $125M, and Fantastic Four at $118M. So the question mostly comes down to a few unreleased late-2025 tentpoles. Zootopia 2 looks like the biggest threat, with one October report saying it was tracking at $155M-$190M, while Avatar: Fire and Ash and Wicked: For Good are also credible challengers. Because there are multiple remaining shots at the title, I make NO a slight favorite overall, but Minecraft still has a substantial chance to hold on as the year’s top opening.

Google is a serious contender but not my favorite as of 2025-10-26. The bullish case is that Google already proved it can lead Chatbot Arena in 2025 with Gemini 2.5 Pro, appears to test many private variants on Arena, and is still improving Gemini via Deep Think. The bearish case is stronger for this specific horizon: multiple August 2025 reports indicate GPT-5 had taken the top/default LM Arena leaderboard position, and I found no comparably strong late-October evidence that Google had regained the public text-leaderboard lead. With only about two months from cutoff to resolution, current/near-current leaderboard incumbency matters a lot. So Google looks like a strong second-place type outcome—very live, but below even odds.

WHO uses the term 'pandemic' sparingly, and no official WHO 2025 page or major pre-cutoff reporting found any disease declared a pandemic by late October. The main plausible candidates look short of that threshold: mpox was still being handled as a PHEIC/emergency-committee matter rather than a pandemic, H5N1 still lacked sustained human-to-human transmission as of Oct. 17 and WHO called risk low, and Ebola/Marburg outbreaks were being contained rather than escalating globally. With only about two months left in 2025, YES would likely require a sudden major international respiratory spread plus WHO choosing to use the specific word 'pandemic' quickly. That seems possible but unlikely.

As of the 2025-10-26 cutoff, Starmer was still clearly serving as PM and carrying out normal official duties in late October. There is genuine political danger around him: major Labour rebellions, poor polling, and open leadership chatter involving Andy Burnham. But for this market to resolve YES, he must cease to be PM or announce departure by Dec. 31, leaving only about two months. The evidence available by the cutoff points more toward medium-term vulnerability than an imminent year-end exit: no formal challenge is evident, Labour’s rules impose a significant nomination hurdle, no obvious successor is ready, and even critical coverage often pointed to 2026 as the likelier danger window. So I put the remaining 2025 hazard in the low-teens.

John Smith is a confirmed entrant in a 64-player single-elimination field, so the generic base rate is only about 1.6%. Pre-cutoff reporting does suggest he is stronger than average in this exact format: he was described as a heads-up specialist / 'King of the Heads-Up Tournaments' and was reported to have finished runner-up in the WSOP Heads-Up Championship in both 2016 and 2017. That said, he is 78 years old, the field is stacked with elite modern pros, and winning requires six straight match wins. On balance, he looks like a legitimate but still clear longshot contender, so I land at the floor-consistent estimate of 5%.

Z.ai is a credible frontier challenger, but the pre-cutoff evidence is much stronger for 'serious contender' than for 'current Chatbot Arena leader.' Z.ai had meaningful momentum from GLM-4.5 and GLM-4.6, yet I found no direct evidence that a Z.ai model was actually on top of Chatbot Arena close to Oct. 26. Meanwhile, public discussion pointed to rivals such as OpenAI already topping LMArena earlier in 2025, and third-party coverage of GLM-4.5 placed it behind top OpenAI/Anthropic systems on other benchmark bundles. Given only a 5-day horizon, Z.ai likely needs either to already be very near #1 or to ship a late-breaking upgrade; both are plausible but not the base case. The alphabetical tie-break also hurts Z.ai in any exact tie. Net: underdog, but live.

I estimate a 14% chance. The key outside-view point is that a nominal >$250B Q4/Q4 drop in federal current expenditures is uncommon because federal spending is sticky and entitlement/interest costs generally rise. The strongest inside-view evidence by the 2025-10-26 cutoff is adverse: CBO reported adjusted FY2025 outlays were still running above FY2024, including +$247B through June and +$228B for the full fiscal year after timing adjustments, which is directionally inconsistent with the sort of large decline needed here. There is still some chance because NIPA current expenditures are not identical to budget outlays and one-off accounting items (for example student-loan/subsidy changes) can move federal spending measures substantially, but absent direct BEA evidence of a sharp late-2025 drop, YES looks unlikely.

The album is off to an exceptionally strong start: it was released, debuted at No. 1, had a record 4.002M-unit opening, stayed massively ahead in week 2, and appears likely to have a third straight week banked by the cutoff (search_0_result_1, search_1_summary, search_2_result_2). Late-2025 announced competition also looked relatively light as of Oct. 19 (search_4_summary). But the bar here is not 'many weeks at No. 1'—it is 17 consecutive weeks, which is historically extreme. Swift’s own TTPD reached 17 total weeks at No. 1 only with returns after an initial 12-week streak, and historical comps suggest 17 straight from debut would be unprecedented or nearly so (search_5_summary). Given those cross-pressures, I land at 26%.

DeepSeek is a real contender, but the best available late-2025 evidence points away from it holding the single top Chatbot Arena slot on Dec. 31. The strongest signal is that Google’s Gemini 2.5 Pro was reported #1 on Chatbot Arena in late August 2025 with a large 35-Elo lead over second place, while Google also showed strong benchmark momentum with Gemini 2.5 Deep Think. DeepSeek did keep releasing models, but its late-September V3.2-Exp was described as an efficiency-focused intermediate step that was roughly on par in general performance with V3.1, not an obvious Arena-topping leap. That said, DeepSeek remained in the frontier set and still had some chance to launch a stronger next-generation model before year-end; the alphabetical tiebreak also slightly helps it versus Google/OpenAI/xAI. Net: plausible but clearly an underdog.

My estimate is slightly above even. By the Oct. 26 cutoff, the best evidence suggests Bitcoin had not yet broken $100k during the market window: Reuters’ reported October washout only reached about $104.8k, and CoinDesk said BTC had held above $100k for four consecutive months. But late-October BTC was only about 5-7% above the trigger level (~$105k on Oct. 17 and mid-$107k on Oct. 22), and the market only needs a brief 1-minute Binance wick below $100k, not a sustained close. Combined with ongoing macro pressure and heavy put/hedging activity concentrated at the $100k strike, that makes a sub-$100k dip before year-end a bit more likely than not, though strong support at $100k keeps it close to a coin flip.

Musk is a real contender but not the likeliest one as of 2025-10-26. He had major early-year influence via DOGE and remains central to AI/SpaceX/X narratives, but authoritative Reuters reporting says his political influence waned by midyear and late-October odds/market coverage consistently placed Trump and/or AI ahead of him. Since TIME usually picks the clearest symbol of the year—and Musk already won in 2021—his path looks live but distinctly secondary.

I estimate a 27% chance. The YES case is much stronger than for a typical album because this is Taylor Swift, the album already debuted at #1, and it opened with an unprecedented ~4 million U.S. units before holding a second week at #1 with 338,000 units amid weak immediate competition. But 16 consecutive weeks is an extremely high bar: no 21st-century album appears to have reached that mark, and even Swift’s strongest direct precedent, The Tortured Poets Department, managed 12 straight weeks before being interrupted. So this looks plausible but still clearly less likely than not.

By 2025-10-26, the clearest path to YES requires Zootopia 2 to beat the existing 2025 leader, A Minecraft Movie, which opened around $157M final / $163M estimate domestically. The most authoritative late-October trade forecast I found (Boxoffice Pro) had Zootopia 2 at only $95M-$110M for the 3-day and $140M-$160M for the 5-day Thanksgiving launch. That gap matters because this market resolves on the Fri-Sun 3-day figure, and Thanksgiving releases tend to look stronger on 5-day than on pure 3-day. Some other reports were more bullish at roughly $115M-$130M, which means Zootopia 2 is a real contender for one of the year's biggest openings, but it still looks notably short of the number it likely needs. Additional competition from Wicked: For Good and later Avatar: Fire and Ash trims the path a bit further, though Minecraft already looks like the main hurdle. Net: YES is plausible only via a substantial overperformance versus tracking, so I put it in the mid-teens rather than near-even.

By the Oct. 26 cutoff, the weight of public evidence leans to Tyler Robinson being a lone actor. The strongest pro-YES evidence is that reporting tied to the charging document said he acted alone and that nobody around him appeared to know beforehand, and Gov. Spencer Cox also said he was believed to have acted alone. The main counterevidence is that the FBI later said it was still investigating the possibility of accomplices. But that looks more like ongoing due diligence than an affirmative indication that collaborators existed: I found no authoritative pre-cutoff reporting of any accomplice being charged, publicly named, or clearly implicated, and later court coverage continued to center on Robinson as the sole defendant. Because the market can resolve on what law enforcement believe most likely even absent a definitive statement, the early official lone-actor framing matters a lot. Still, the FBI’s public caution keeps this well below certainty.

OpenAI looks like a serious contender but not the favorite. By August 2025, GPT-5 reportedly reached #1 on LMArena, so OpenAI clearly had a model capable of topping the board. But leadership was rotating among several frontier labs during 2025, and by late October there was still evidence of very strong rival performance, including Gemini earlier in the year and GLM-4.6 leading Arena's open-model rankings. With only about two months left, OpenAI has a plausible path to regain or hold #1, but the field appears crowded and the tie-break rule is unfavorable to OpenAI against Google. Net: roughly a one-in-three chance.

By the Oct. 26 cutoff, the best available evidence suggests October’s low was still around $104.8k and BTC had rebounded/consolidated near $111k by Oct. 25. That leaves only a few days for a further ~19% drop to print a Binance 1-minute low at or below $90k. Bitcoin can have violent tail moves, and the market had just suffered a record crash with heavy downside hedging, so YES is not negligible. But the weight of evidence points to downside strikes centered more around $95k-$100k than $90k, making a sub-$90k October print unlikely but possible.

Francesca Muñoz appears extremely unlikely to win. By late October, credible coverage consistently framed the race around José Antonio Kast and Jeannette Jara, with Matthei as the other major contender, not Muñoz. More decisively, multiple June 30 reports indicate Muñoz withdrew her presidential candidacy and endorsed Kast. If she was no longer an active candidate, her chance of ultimately winning is effectively near zero; under the required floor, I submit 5%.

I estimate a 10% chance of YES. By the Oct. 26 cutoff, there was clearly a live U.S.-Venezuela crisis: Reuters reported repeated U.S. military strikes on vessels off Venezuela, provocative Venezuelan F-16 passes over a U.S. destroyer, and a major additional U.S. regional deployment. But the crucial qualifier for this market is still missing in credible reporting: the struck boats were described as suspected drug/civilian vessels, not clearly Venezuelan military forces, and I found no confirmed exchange of fire with FANB or U.S. strike on Venezuelan land. With only five days left in the window, most remaining probability comes from accidental escalation or from an ambiguous prior incident later being shown to involve Venezuelan military assets.

Valencia look like a very remote long shot as of 2025-10-26. The outside view is brutal: modern La Liga titles are overwhelmingly won by Real Madrid, Barcelona, or Atlético. The inside view points the same way: Opta’s preseason model had Valencia only 10th on 49 points, not remotely title level (search_0_result_4); mid-October betting coverage centered the title race on Barcelona/Real with Villarreal as the main outsider, while Valencia were not even listed among the main longshots (search_0_result_2, search_3_result_9); and Valencia had already taken a 6-0 loss to Barcelona early in the season (search_2_result_4). Reporting around Oct. 16 also had them in the bottom half of the table, so by the forecast date they were not in serious contention (search_3_result_4). Given the platform floor, I submit 5%; my true estimate is plausibly lower.

By the cutoff, this outcome is effectively already dead. Multiple pre-cutoff sources, including Billboard, report that Taylor Swift’s 'The Life of a Showgirl' debuted at No. 1 on the Billboard 200 (chart dated Oct. 18, 2025). Since the 0-weeks bracket applies only if it never reaches No. 1 (or is unreleased by Dec. 31, 2025), a confirmed No. 1 debut makes 0 straight weeks essentially impossible absent some highly unusual chart reversal. I therefore put the YES probability at the floor.

I put Drake at 11%. The key reason is that the best pre-cutoff proxy for 2025 Spotify artist totals had him only 4th in the annual race, behind Bad Bunny, Taylor Swift, and The Weeknd, with Bad Bunny specifically shown at 21.00B streams and Drake down a spot from last year (search_4_result_0). Drake did have a meaningful 2025 boost from the PartyNextDoor collab album and viral tracks like 'Nokia,' but the evidence suggests a solid year, not the kind of runaway dominance usually needed for Spotify’s global #1 artist (search_1_result_0, search_1_result_8). Wrapped also appears to keep counting somewhat past Oct. 31, so there was still some runway left at the forecast date, but probably not enough for a 4th-place artist to overtake multiple leaders absent a huge late surge (search_2_result_0, search_2_result_1, search_2_result_2).

As of 2025-10-26, a YES would require a non-nuclear US ally to move from non-possession on March 10 to independently controlled operational possession by Dec. 31, with credible confirmation. That is an extremely rare event and the remaining time is short. The realistic candidate pool had narrowed mainly to Japan and South Korea, with Poland/Germany discussion centered on extended deterrence or nuclear sharing, which would not count. Reporting through October showed debate, hedging, and worsening security concerns, but not confirmed acquisition or even clear evidence of an active breakout program. South Korea still faced key fuel-cycle constraints, while Japan had greater latent capability but official policy still emphasized strengthening deterrence within the alliance rather than acquiring weapons. I therefore put this at the floor-ish probability: possible in a tail-risk covert/crash scenario, but very unlikely.

By the Oct. 26, 2025 cutoff, RFK Jr. had repeatedly promised autism-cause answers, which keeps some chance alive because the market resolves on his public announcement rather than scientific consensus. But the strongest late evidence points the other way: he reportedly moved the target to around March 2026, and the official Sept. 22 HHS autism event announced only 'progress,' association studies, and new research funding—not that a definitive cause had been identified. With only five days left before Oct. 31 and no evidence of an imminent late-October release, YES looks unlikely though not impossible given Kennedy’s willingness to make bold claims.

Peru’s chance is effectively zero by the 2025-10-26 cutoff because the available September 2025 reporting indicates they were already mathematically eliminated from CONMEBOL qualification after Matchday 17: Peru had 12 points, while 7th-place Venezuela had 18 with only one match remaining, so Peru could no longer reach even the intercontinental playoff spot (search_4_summary). Additional September reports explicitly described Peru as eliminated after losing 3-0 to Uruguay (search_0_result_0, search_1_result_2). Since a team that cannot qualify cannot win the 2026 World Cup, this should resolve NO; I am submitting the minimum allowed 5% only because the tool instructions prohibit lower values.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the best available evidence pointed strongly to 2025 finishing below 2024. Across major datasets, 2025 was running second-warmest year-to-date, not first; Jan-Aug was about 0.12°C cooler than 2024, and September 2025 was only the third-warmest September, still below September 2024. Carbon Brief synthesis said 2025 was on track to be second or third warmest and 'very unlikely' to beat 2024, while NOAA's annual outlook put the chance of 2025 ending as warmest at under 1% in its dataset. Late-2025 ENSO expectations also leaned weak-La-Niña-ish, which is not the backdrop you want for a dramatic catch-up. I keep a small residual probability because the market resolves on NASA GISTEMP specifically, not NOAA/Copernicus, and cross-dataset differences plus possible extreme warmth in the last months could still produce an upset. But overall NO is much more likely than YES.

I estimate a 10% chance. The best available pre-cutoff evidence indicates xAI was not leading the relevant leaderboard for most of the period close to resolution: in mid-July 2025, Grok 4 was reported behind Google Gemini 2.5 Pro and OpenAI o3/4o-class models on LMArena (search_2_result_1, search_2_result_2, search_2_result_6). Additional late-summer evidence suggests OpenAI's GPT-5 topped LMArena leaderboards by early August, which is even closer to the Oct. 31 check date and further weakens xAI's position (search_3_result_5). xAI did keep shipping models, including Grok 4 Fast in September, so a last-minute jump is not impossible (search_5_result_1), but that release seems framed more around efficiency/cost than a clear raw-performance leap over the leaders (search_5_result_6). With only five days from forecast date to resolution, current standing should dominate, so xAI looks like a minority contender rather than the likeliest winner.

By the Oct. 26 cutoff, the strongest and most recent evidence points to Tesla not yet having launched a qualifying unsupervised FSD product/service. Tesla had made Austin robotaxi rides more publicly accessible and some rides reportedly had no one in the driver’s seat, which keeps a narrow YES path alive (search_1_summary; search_0_summary). But the key disqualifier is that late-October reporting still described human safety monitors in Tesla robotaxis, with a kill switch, and Musk said removal of those monitors was expected only 'by the end of the year'—after the Oct. 31 deadline (search_1_summary; search_1_result_5; search_5_result_0). Separately, Tesla’s own framing and July guidance pointed to supervised/current operations and unsupervised customer use only by year-end, not by Oct. 31 (search_0_summary; search_3_result_2). Given the strict resolution criteria, especially no required human oversight/intervention readiness, the probability of a qualifying launch in the final five days looks low but not impossible.

By the 2025-10-26 cutoff, the Bucharest by-election is definitely scheduled for Dec. 7, but Victor Ponta is not showing up in the visible candidate field or in the race polling I found. Early post-vacancy coverage named other serious contenders—Ciucu, Băluță, Drulă, Firea, Piedone, Vlad Gheorghe, Berceanu—while Ponta was absent (search_3_summary). Late-October coverage again focused on Băluță, Drulă, and Ciucu, with a CURS poll putting them at 25/18/18 and still no mention of Ponta as candidate or poll subject (search_4_summary). Since the candidacy deadline was still ahead on Nov. 17, there remains some chance of a late Ponta entry, but as of the cutoff he looked outside the active field. So YES is possible, but unlikely.

September already delivered the first required cut, and late-October evidence made an October cut look extremely likely as well. But the same late-October Reuters/CME and Reuters economist-poll evidence pointed strongly toward yet another December cut, not a pause. So the contract mostly depends on a relatively small chance that the Fed cuts in October and then unexpectedly holds in December. I put that tail around high single digits.

I estimate a low chance. The key driver is distance-to-target versus time remaining: multiple pre-cutoff sources place SOL around roughly $192-$209 in late October 2025, which would require about a 70%+ move in under a week to print $350 on Binance (search_0_result_1). In addition, several sources indicate Solana’s 2025/ATH peak was about $293.31 in January, so $350 would require a substantial new all-time high rather than merely revisiting prior levels (search_0_result_3; search_3_summary). Reuters reporting also described a sharp mid-October crypto drawdown, with Solana dropping nearly 22% in two days and flows weakening afterward, which is not the backdrop most conducive to an immediate blow-off move by month-end (search_2_summary). Against that, there was still meaningful institutional interest and some market belief in a strong end-of-year run for SOL, so a tail-risk upside spike cannot be ruled out entirely (search_2_summary; search_3_summary). Given the short horizon, the very large required move, and the fact that $350 sat well above known 2025 highs, I put the chance at 6%.

Bad Bunny clearly remained a live contender in 2025 thanks to a major January release cycle, but the balance of evidence by 2025-10-26 still points away from him being the eventual Spotify Wrapped #1. He had a strong start: 'Debí Tirar Más Fotos' opened at No. 1 on Billboard’s Top Streaming Albums and delivered a very large streaming-driven Billboard 200 week. But Taylor Swift was the incumbent top Spotify artist from 2023 and 2024, and by early October 2025 she reportedly added another record-breaking Spotify moment with a new album and single that set single-day streaming marks. Without a direct authoritative year-to-date ranking showing Bad Bunny ahead by late October, the safer forecast is that he trails the field leader, most likely Taylor Swift, though his chance remains meaningful because of his huge global catalog and strong 2025 album performance.

Biedermann’s 1:42.00 was still the listed LCM 200m freestyle WR on 2025-10-26, and the available pre-cutoff context points to a record that remains very hard to reach in the near term. July 2025 coverage described the men’s 200 free as an event where observers were unsure anyone would even need 1:43 to win Worlds, which leaves meaningful daylight to 1:42.00. The best-known modern threat is David Popovici’s 1:42.97 textile-suit swim—close enough to keep some risk alive, but still nearly a full second short. The forecast window is also mostly outside the main long-course championship season, which reduces the number of highly optimized record opportunities. So I make YES very likely on all three dates, with a gradual decline as the horizon extends.

Wikipedia as of the cutoff still showed the 1 Oct 2025 list, with Hans Niemann at world #15 on 2738. That is encouraging, but the fetched table also shows how fragile the cutoff is: ranks 15, 16, 18 were all on 2738 and rank 17 was on 2737, so a 1-point swing can move several places. The one search result I used adds two offsetting signals: Chess.com described #15/2738 as a new career-best, implying genuine recent momentum, but also a level near the top of his recent range; and 2700chess/US Championship results show he was still playing rated games in late October, so the Nov. 1 list could move materially. For 2025-11-02 I give a higher probability because Wikipedia may still have lagged on updating from the October list, and even if updated he had some momentum. By 2025-11-25 the page was more likely to reflect the November list, so the probability drops. By 2026-01-24 there is more time for both Niemann and nearby rivals to move, so I pull the estimate closer to 50%.
